conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out. I'll help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To assess this, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who is 6, and Blake, who is 3. They both live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3) who both live with you.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you. Now, is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions to ensure I have all the information needed.

Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I understand Blake has been in hospital for about 3 months.

Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital - for example, on clothes, toys, or other items for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I have just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Blake in a UK hospital, or is he in a hospital abroad?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And finally, does Blake spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or has he been staying in the hospital continuously?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's been staying in the hospital continuously - he hasn't been coming home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Alex (age 6):** ✓ **You are eligible for Child Benefit**

Alex meets all the standard eligibility requirements. He is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

**For Blake (age 3):** ✗ **You are not eligible for Child Benefit (after the 12-week period)**

Unfortunately, because Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), your entitlement to Child Benefit for Blake would end after that 12-week period. The rules state that Child Benefit is affected when a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks. 

There are exceptions that can allow Child Benefit to continue, such as regularly spending money on the child while they're in hospital, or if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. However, since Blake has been staying in hospital continuously and you haven't been able to spend money on his needs during this time, these exceptions don't apply in your situation.

**Important:** You should inform HMRC about Blake's hospital stay. I understand this is a difficult situation, and I hope Blake recovers soon. If circumstances change - for example, if you're able to start regularly spending money on Blake's needs, or if he starts coming home for visits - you should contact HMRC as this could affect your eligibility.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. The claimant is responsible for Alex who lives with them. Alex meets all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: the child is under 16, lives in the UK with the claimant, and no one else is claiming for them.

Blake is 3 years old and normally lives with the claimant in the UK. However, Blake has been in a UK hospital continuously for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks). According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. After the 12-week limit, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply. The exceptions include: regularly spending money on the child while they're in hospital. The claimant has confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while in hospital, and Blake has not been spending at least 24 hours a week at home. Therefore, no exceptions apply, and the claimant would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake after the 12-week period has passed.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex meets all standard eligibility criteria. However, Blake has been in a UK hospital continuously for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks). Under the rules, Child Benefit entitlement typically ends after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, unless exceptions apply (such as regularly spending money on the child or the child spending at least 24 hours per week at home). The claimant has not been able to spend money on Blake while in hospital, and Blake has been staying continuously without coming home. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake after the 12-week hospital period.
